settingDescription: A market town of roughly 2,400 souls in the foothills east of the Rhine. Grown from grazing settlements around a river crossing, Schafweide rose to regional significance on the back of its wool trade. The weekly Thursday market on the Marktplatz is its heartbeat. Three guild families — the Brenners, the Kesslers, and the Hartmanns — govern through a council that presents unity while nursing quiet rivalries. A monastery on the hill above the Marktplatz holds the town's literacy and its conscience. Six months ago, a printing press arrived.

institutions.name: The Hartmann Mill and River Crossing

personsOfInterest.name: Heinrich Voss
personsOfInterest.role: Printer and proprietor of Der Weidenbote

initialSieveEntries.description: The Benedictine brothers of Saint Aegidius established a monastery on the hill above what would become the Marktplatz, drawn by the river crossing and the sparse settlement of shepherds already gathered there. The brothers brought literacy, record-keeping, and the first permanent stone structure in the region.
initialSieveEntries.summary: Monastery of Saint Aegidius founded on the hill above Schafweide by Benedictine brothers.
initialSieveEntries.fragment: The monastery predates the town itself.

initialSieveEntries.description: The Brenner family formalized the Thursday wool market into a chartered guild, securing regional trading rights from the local imperial administrator. The charter gave the Brenners legal precedence over wool pricing and market access — an authority they have held, and quietly expanded, ever since.
initialSieveEntries.summary: Brenner family chartered the Wool Guild, securing regional trading rights.
initialSieveEntries.fragment: The Brenner Wool Guild charter predates the council by two generations.

initialSieveEntries.description: The Kessler family established the Tanners Guild following a prolonged dispute with the Brenners over market stall placement. The tanners' trade — considered unehrlich by many for its association with dead animals and foul-smelling processes — was granted a secondary charter with restricted market access. The Kesslers have never forgotten the subordinate terms of their founding.
initialSieveEntries.summary: Kessler Tanners Guild founded under restricted charter following dispute with Brenner Wool Guild. The tanners' unehrlich status embedded into founding terms.
initialSieveEntries.fragment: The Kessler guild was founded in grievance and has operated in it ever since.

initialSieveEntries.description: The Hartmann family financed and built a permanent stone bridge over the river crossing that had previously been a seasonal ford. By controlling the only reliable crossing in the region, the Hartmanns secured a toll revenue stream and a stranglehold on commerce entering and leaving Schafweide from the east.
initialSieveEntries.summary: Hartmann family built permanent stone bridge, securing toll revenue and commercial chokehold on eastern trade routes.
initialSieveEntries.fragment: The Hartmann bridge is the reason Schafweide grew and the reason the Hartmanns matter.

initialSieveEntries.description: The three dominant families — Brenner, Kessler, and Hartmann — formalized their arrangement into a governing council following a season of disputes over market taxation and bridge toll revenues. The council presented itself to the population as unified civic governance. In practice it was, and remains, a negotiated ceasefire between three competing commercial interests.
initialSieveEntries.summary: The Council of Schafweide formed by the three guild families as a formalized power-sharing arrangement.
initialSieveEntries.fragment: The council was never unity. It was always a ceasefire.

initialSieveEntries.description: Heinrich Voss, a minor merchant with no guild affiliation, returned from Mainz with a printing press and established Der Weidenbote — The Pasture Messenger — in a rented ground-floor room on the east side of the Marktplatz. His first publication, a broadsheet reporting wool prices across four regional markets, sold out in an afternoon. The monastery has not yet decided how to respond. The Brenner family has already sent a representative to discuss arrangements.
initialSieveEntries.summary: Heinrich Voss established Der Weidenbote printing press on the Marktplatz. First broadsheet reported regional wool prices, immediately attracting guild and Church attention.
initialSieveEntries.fragment: The press arrived in October 1466. Nothing in Schafweide has been entirely the same since.
